era_context: The early 1780s marked the climax of the Age of Revolutions and the late Enlightenment. The American War of Independence was reaching its decisive phase, drawing in France, Spain, and the Dutch Republic against Britain in a global conflict stretching from the Caribbean to India. In South America, the Spanish Empire faced the largest indigenous uprising in its history. The Qing dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor remained the world's most populous polity, while the Ottoman Empire navigated between European pressures and internal reform. Enlightenment science was accelerating, with astronomy, chemistry, and natural philosophy producing landmark discoveries.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading posts
Aboriginal Australian societies prior to sustained European contact
Pacific Island communities beyond initial European exploration accounts
Central Asian steppe polities between Qing and Russian spheres
Interior Southeast Asian kingdoms beyond court chronicles

events.title: Siege of Yorktown and British surrender
events.description: From 28 September to 19 October 1781, combined American and French forces under General George Washington and the Comte de Rochambeau besieged a British army under Lieutenant General Charles Cornwallis at Yorktown, Virginia. A French fleet under the Comte de Grasse blocked British naval relief after the decisive Battle of the Chesapeake (5 September). Cornwallis surrendered approximately 7,000 troops on 19 October. Though the Treaty of Paris was not signed until 1783, Yorktown effectively ended major combat operations and ensured American independence.
key_figures: George Washington (Commander-in-Chief, Continental Army)
Comte de Rochambeau (Commander, French expeditionary force)
Comte de Grasse (Commander, French fleet)
Charles Cornwallis (British commander at Yorktown)
Alexander Hamilton (led assault on Redoubt No. 10)

sources.name: Washington's correspondence and general orders, October 1781 (Papers of George Washington)
Cornwallis's capitulation articles, 19 October 1781
Richard Ketchum, Victory at Yorktown: The Campaign That Won the Revolution (2004)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by both sides; exact dates confirmed by multiple contemporary sources.
cross_references: 1783-TREATY_PARIS (Treaty of Paris formally ended the war)

events.title: Battle of the Chesapeake (Battle of the Virginia Capes)
events.region: Chesapeake Bay, Virginia, British North America
events.description: On 5 September 1781, a French fleet of 24 ships of the line under the Comte de Grasse engaged a British fleet of 19 ships under Rear Admiral Thomas Graves off the mouth of Chesapeake Bay. The tactically indecisive but strategically devastating engagement prevented the Royal Navy from relieving Cornwallis at Yorktown. Graves withdrew to New York for repairs, ceding naval control of the bay. Historians widely regard this as one of the most consequential naval battles in world history, as it sealed the fate of the British position at Yorktown.
key_figures: Comte de Grasse (French fleet commander)
Thomas Graves (British fleet commander)
Samuel Hood (British rear division commander)

sources.name: De Grasse's dispatch to Rochambeau, September 1781
Graves's after-action report to the Admiralty
Jonathan Dull, The French Navy and American Independence (1975)

events.certainty_note: Date and details confirmed by multiple contemporary naval records from both fleets.

events.title: Articles of Confederation ratified
events.description: On 1 March 1781, the Articles of Confederation were formally ratified after Maryland became the last state to approve them, establishing the first constitutional framework for the United States. The Articles created a weak central government with no power to tax, regulate commerce, or enforce laws upon the states. While they provided a legal basis for congressional governance during the war, their structural deficiencies became increasingly apparent and ultimately led to the Constitutional Convention of 1787.
key_figures: John Hanson (elected first President of the Congress under the Articles)
John Dickinson (principal drafter of the original 1776–77 draft)

sources.name: Journals of the Continental Congress, 1 March 1781
Merrill Jensen, The Articles of Confederation (1940; reprinted 1959)

events.certainty_note: Ratification date documented in official congressional records.

events.title: William Herschel discovers the planet Uranus
events.description: On 13 March 1781, the German-born astronomer William Herschel observed an object in the constellation Gemini using a homemade 6.2-inch reflecting telescope from his garden in Bath, England. Initially believing it to be a comet, subsequent calculations by Anders Johan Lexell and others confirmed it as the seventh planet of the solar system. It was the first planet discovered in recorded history — all others visible to the naked eye had been known since antiquity. Herschel named it 'Georgium Sidus' after King George III; the name Uranus, proposed by Johann Elert Bode, eventually prevailed internationally.
key_figures: William Herschel (astronomer)
Anders Johan Lexell (mathematician who computed the orbit)
Johann Elert Bode (proposed the name Uranus)

sources.name: William Herschel, 'Account of a Comet', Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society, vol. 71 (1781)
Michael Hoskin, The Herschel Partnership (2003)

events.certainty_note: Observation date confirmed by Herschel's own journal and published paper; orbital confirmation documented in contemporary correspondence.

events.title: Execution of Túpac Amaru II and collapse of the Great Rebellion
events.description: On 18 May 1781, José Gabriel Condorcanqui, who had taken the name Túpac Amaru II, was publicly executed in Cuzco along with his wife Micaela Bastidas and other leaders of the rebellion. The uprising, which had erupted in November 1780, was the largest anti-colonial revolt in Spanish American history, mobilising tens of thousands of indigenous and mestizo people across Peru and Upper Peru (modern Bolivia). Though the main leadership was destroyed, fighting continued in Upper Peru under Túpac Katari and others into late 1781. Spain responded with harsh repression and administrative reforms, including the suppression of indigenous cultural symbols.
key_figures: Túpac Amaru II / José Gabriel Condorcanqui (rebel leader)
Micaela Bastidas (co-leader, executed)
Visitador General José Antonio de Areche (Spanish official who oversaw the trial)
Túpac Katari (Aymara leader who continued resistance in Upper Peru)

sources.name: Trial records and sentencing documents from the Audiencia of Cuzco, 1781
Charles Walker, The Tupac Amaru Rebellion (2014)
Scarlett O'Phelan Godoy, Rebellions and Revolts in Eighteenth-Century Peru and Upper Peru (1985)

events.certainty_note: Execution date and details confirmed by contemporary Spanish colonial records and trial documentation.

events.title: Siege of La Paz by Túpac Katari
events.region: La Paz, Viceroyalty of Peru (modern Bolivia)
events.description: Between March and October 1781, the Aymara leader Julián Apaza, known as Túpac Katari, besieged the city of La Paz twice with forces numbering in the tens of thousands. The siege caused severe famine among the city's inhabitants and came close to overwhelming the Spanish garrison. Relief forces eventually lifted the siege, and Túpac Katari was captured and executed by quartering on 15 November 1781. His reported final words — 'I will return as millions' — became a foundational statement of indigenous resistance movements in Bolivia, though the quotation's authenticity is debated.
key_figures: Túpac Katari / Julián Apaza (Aymara rebel leader)
Bartolina Sisa (Katari's wife, military co-leader)
Sebastián de Segurola (Spanish commander defending La Paz)

sources.name: Contemporary Spanish military dispatches and siege accounts from La Paz
Sinclair Thomson, We Alone Will Rule: Native Andean Politics in the Age of Insurgency (2002)

events.certainty_note: Siege dates and Katari's execution well attested in colonial records; the famous last words are of uncertain authenticity.

events.title: Battle of Cowpens
events.description: On 17 January 1781, American forces under Brigadier General Daniel Morgan decisively defeated a British force under Lieutenant Colonel Banastre Tarleton at the Cowpens in upcountry South Carolina. Morgan employed a brilliant double-envelopment tactic using militia and Continental regulars in coordinated lines. The British suffered over 800 casualties and captured, compared to roughly 70 American casualties. The victory shattered Tarleton's feared legion and was a turning point in the Southern campaign, forcing Cornwallis into the aggressive northward pursuit that eventually trapped him at Yorktown.
key_figures: Daniel Morgan (American brigadier general)
Banastre Tarleton (British lieutenant colonel)

sources.name: Morgan's after-action report to Nathanael Greene, January 1781
Lawrence Babits, A Devil of a Whipping: The Battle of Cowpens (1998)

events.certainty_note: Date and details confirmed by multiple contemporary reports from both American and British officers.

events.title: Battle of Guilford Court House
events.description: On 15 March 1781, British forces under Cornwallis engaged the American Southern Army under Major General Nathanael Greene at Guilford Court House, North Carolina. Although Cornwallis held the field — a technical British victory — his army suffered approximately 25% casualties, losses he could not replace far from his supply base. The pyrrhic victory prompted Charles James Fox to quip in Parliament that 'another such victory would ruin the British army.' Cornwallis abandoned the Carolinas and marched north into Virginia, the strategic decision that led to his entrapment at Yorktown.
key_figures: Charles Cornwallis (British commander)
Nathanael Greene (American commander)

sources.name: Cornwallis's dispatch to Lord Germain, 17 March 1781
John Buchanan, The Road to Guilford Courthouse (1997)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented engagement; date and casualty figures confirmed by both sides' records.

events.title: Fourth Anglo-Dutch War: Battle of Dogger Bank
events.description: On 5 August 1781, a British squadron under Vice Admiral Hyde Parker engaged a Dutch squadron under Rear Admiral Johan Zoutman at Dogger Bank in the North Sea. The fierce but inconclusive engagement was the only major fleet action of the Fourth Anglo-Dutch War (1780–1784), which had begun over Dutch trade with the American rebels. Both fleets were badly damaged. The war exposed the decline of Dutch naval power and contributed to the political crisis of the Dutch Republic, eventually feeding into the Patriot movement of the 1780s.
key_figures: Hyde Parker (British vice admiral)
Johan Zoutman (Dutch rear admiral)

sources.name: Contemporary Admiralty dispatches and Dutch naval records
Jonathan Israel, The Dutch Republic: Its Rise, Greatness and Fall (1995)

events.certainty_note: Date and engagement details confirmed by both British and Dutch naval records.

events.title: Kant publishes Critique of Pure Reason
events.description: In 1781, Immanuel Kant published the Kritik der reinen Vernunft (Critique of Pure Reason), one of the most influential works in the history of Western philosophy. The work argued that human knowledge is shaped by the structures of the mind itself — that space, time, and causality are forms imposed by cognition rather than features of things-in-themselves. It sought to resolve the impasse between rationalism and empiricism and effectively redefined the scope and method of philosophy. A substantially revised second edition appeared in 1787.
key_figures: Immanuel Kant (philosopher)

sources.name: Immanuel Kant, Critik der reinen Vernunft (Riga: Johann Friedrich Hartknoch, 1781)
Paul Guyer (ed.), The Cambridge Companion to Kant (1992)

events.certainty_note: Publication date confirmed; first edition appeared in May 1781 from publisher Hartknoch in Riga.

events.title: Hyder Ali and the Second Anglo-Mysore War
events.description: The Second Anglo-Mysore War (1780–1784) continued in 1781 with Hyder Ali of Mysore, allied with the French, pressing campaigns against British East India Company forces in the Carnatic. British forces under Sir Eyre Coote won the Battle of Porto Novo on 1 July 1781, checking Hyder Ali's advance toward Madras (Chennai), followed by further engagements at Pollilur (August) and Sholinghur (September). Despite these tactical setbacks, Hyder Ali maintained strategic pressure, and the war demonstrated the vulnerability of British territorial gains in India to determined Indian opposition supported by European allies.
key_figures: Hyder Ali (Sultan of Mysore)
Sir Eyre Coote (British East India Company general)
Tipu Sultan (Hyder Ali's son, commanding forces)

sources.name: East India Company military records and dispatches, 1781
Mohibbul Hasan, History of Tipu Sultan (1951; revised editions)
G.J. Bryant, The Emergence of British Power in India, 1600–1784 (2013)

events.certainty_note: Battle dates and outcomes confirmed by both British East India Company records and Mysorean sources.

events.title: Spain captures Pensacola from Britain
events.description: On 10 May 1781, a Spanish force of over 7,000 under Bernardo de Gálvez captured the British garrison at Pensacola after a two-month siege, completing Spain's conquest of British West Florida. The victory secured Spanish control over the Gulf Coast from New Orleans to the Florida panhandle, removing the British military presence from the Gulf of Mexico. Gálvez's campaigns, often overlooked in American Revolution narratives, constituted a significant contribution to the Allied war effort by tying down British resources and eliminating a base for potential counterattacks.
key_figures: Bernardo de Gálvez (Governor of Spanish Louisiana, commander)
John Campbell (British garrison commander at Pensacola)

sources.name: Gálvez's dispatches to José de Gálvez (Minister of the Indies), 1781
Kathleen DuVal, Independence Lost: Lives on the Edge of the American Revolution (2015)

events.certainty_note: Date confirmed by both Spanish and British military records; surrender terms survive.

events.title: Joseph II issues the Toleranzpatent
events.description: On 13 October 1781, Holy Roman Emperor Joseph II issued the Toleranzpatent (Patent of Toleration), granting Lutherans, Calvinists, and Eastern Orthodox Christians limited rights of private worship, civil rights, and access to public office throughout the Habsburg lands. A subsequent edict in January 1782 extended limited toleration to Jews. While falling short of full religious freedom — Catholicism remained the state religion and public worship was restricted — it was one of the most significant acts of the Josephinist reform programme and a landmark in European religious toleration.
key_figures: Joseph II (Holy Roman Emperor, King of Hungary and Bohemia)

sources.name: Toleranzpatent, 13 October 1781 (Austrian State Archives)
T.C.W. Blanning, Joseph II (1994)
Derek Beales, Joseph II, Volume 2: Against the World, 1780–1790 (2009)

events.certainty_note: Original patent survives in Austrian archives; date and provisions precisely documented.
cross_references: 1786-001 (Virginia Statute for Religious Freedom as parallel Enlightenment legislation)

events.title: Joseph II abolishes serfdom in Bohemia and Austrian lands
events.description: On 1 November 1781, Joseph II issued the Serfdom Patent (Untertanenpatent), abolishing personal serfdom (Leibeigenschaft) in the Bohemian and Austrian hereditary lands. Peasants gained the right to marry, move, and choose occupations without their lord's permission. While the reform did not abolish labor obligations (Robot) or transform land tenure — those changes came incrementally and were partly rolled back under Leopold II — it represented a major Enlightenment reform and affected millions of people across Central Europe.
key_figures: Joseph II (Holy Roman Emperor)

sources.name: Serfdom Patent (Untertanenpatent), 1 November 1781 (Austrian State Archives)
Robert A. Kann, A History of the Habsburg Empire, 1526–1918 (1974)

events.certainty_note: Patent date and text confirmed in state archives; implementation varied by region.

events.title: French capture of Tobago
events.description: On 2 June 1781, a French force under the Marquis de Bouillé captured the British-held island of Tobago after a brief campaign. A British relief squadron under Rear Admiral Drake arrived too late and was deterred by the French fleet under de Grasse. The capture of Tobago was part of the broader French Caribbean campaign during the American Revolutionary War, demonstrating France's naval ascendancy in the region during 1781 and foreshadowing the larger Battle of the Saintes in 1782.
key_figures: Marquis de Bouillé (French governor of Martinique)
Comte de Grasse (French admiral providing naval support)

sources.name: French naval dispatches from the Leeward Islands station, 1781
Michael Duffy, Soldiers, Sugar, and Seapower (1987)

events.certainty_note: Capture date confirmed by both French and British records.

events.title: Serfdom reform initiatives under Tây Sơn rule in Vietnam
events.description: By 1781, the Tây Sơn brothers had consolidated control over much of central and southern Vietnam after their uprising beginning in 1771. The Tây Sơn movement, originating as a popular revolt against the Nguyễn lords, redistributed land, opened trade, and challenged the existing feudal order. The period between 1778 and 1783 saw the Tây Sơn consolidate power while the deposed Nguyễn Ánh (the future Emperor Gia Long) mounted a resistance from the Mekong Delta with increasing outside support. Vietnamese historiography treats this as one of the great popular uprisings in the country's history.
key_figures: Nguyễn Nhạc (eldest Tây Sơn brother, self-proclaimed emperor)
Nguyễn Huệ (most militarily talented Tây Sơn brother)
Nguyễn Ánh (exiled Nguyễn lord, future Emperor Gia Long)

sources.name: Đại Việt sử ký tục biên (Continued Records of Đại Việt) — later compilation from court records
George Dutton, The Tây Sơn Uprising: Society and Rebellion in Eighteenth-Century Vietnam (2006)

events.certainty_note: General political situation well-attested; specific events and dates for 1781 within the Tây Sơn movement are less precisely documented than for the war's major battles.

events.title: Battle of Eutaw Springs
events.description: On 8 September 1781, American forces under Nathanael Greene fought a British force under Lieutenant Colonel Alexander Stuart at Eutaw Springs, South Carolina. The battle was the last major engagement in the Carolinas, and though technically inconclusive, the British withdrew to Charleston afterward, effectively ceding the interior of South Carolina. Combined with Greene's earlier actions at Guilford Court House, Hobkirk's Hill, and Ninety-Six, the Eutaw Springs engagement completed the American strategic reconquest of the Southern interior despite Greene never winning a clear tactical victory.
key_figures: Nathanael Greene (American major general)
Alexander Stuart (British lieutenant colonel)

sources.name: Greene's correspondence with the Continental Congress, September 1781
John Buchanan, The Road to Charleston: Nathanael Greene and the American Revolution (2019)

events.certainty_note: Date and outcome confirmed by contemporary military correspondence from both sides.

events.title: Founding of Los Angeles
events.region: Alta California, Viceroyalty of New Spain
events.description: On 4 September 1781, a group of 44 settlers — 11 families of mixed indigenous, African, and Spanish descent — established the pueblo of El Pueblo de la Reina de los Ángeles on the Porciúncula River under the direction of Governor Felipe de Neve, as part of Spain's programme to colonise Alta California. The pueblo was one of several civilian settlements intended to support the nearby military presidio and Franciscan missions. The diverse ethnic composition of the founding settlers, documented in the official census, is frequently cited in discussions of the multicultural origins of what became the second-largest city in the United States.
key_figures: Felipe de Neve (Governor of the Californias)

sources.name: Governor de Neve's founding instructions and settler census, 1781 (Archivo General de Indias, Seville)
William David Estrada, The Los Angeles Plaza: Sacred and Contested Space (2008)

events.certainty_note: Founding date and settler roster confirmed by Spanish colonial administrative records.

events.title: New Pennsylvania Act for Gradual Abolition takes effect
events.description: Pennsylvania's 1780 Act for the Gradual Abolition of Slavery — the first legislative abolition act in the Americas — began producing practical effects in 1781 as the registration of enslaved persons proceeded and the first children born to enslaved mothers after 1 March 1780 were legally designated for eventual freedom (after 28 years of indentured service). The act did not free any currently enslaved person but established the principle that slavery would end generationally. Other Northern states gradually followed Pennsylvania's model through the 1780s and 1790s.
key_figures: George Bryan (Pennsylvania Supreme Executive Council president, key proponent)

sources.name: An Act for the Gradual Abolition of Slavery, Pennsylvania Assembly (1 March 1780)
Gary Nash, Forging Freedom: The Formation of Philadelphia's Black Community, 1720–1840 (1988)

events.certainty_note: Legislative text and registration records survive; 1780 passage date is certain, with 1781 marking implementation.

events.title: Outbreak of the Comunero Revolt in New Granada
events.description: In March 1781, a popular uprising known as the Revolución de los Comuneros erupted in Socorro, New Granada, triggered by new Bourbon tax increases imposed by Regent-Visitor Juan Francisco Gutiérrez de Piñeres. Under the leadership of Juan Francisco Berbeo and José Antonio Galán, a force of some 20,000 marchers advanced toward the capital Bogotá. Spanish authorities negotiated the Capitulaciones de Zipaquirá in June 1781, conceding most demands, but repudiated the agreement once the movement dispersed. Galán was executed in 1782. The revolt is considered a precursor to Colombian independence.
key_figures: Juan Francisco Berbeo (Comunero leader)
José Antonio Galán (radical Comunero leader, later executed)
Juan Francisco Gutiérrez de Piñeres (Spanish regent-visitor whose tax reforms provoked the revolt)
Archbishop Antonio Caballero y Góngora (mediator at Zipaquirá)

sources.name: Capitulaciones de Zipaquirá, June 1781
John Leddy Phelan, The People and the King: The Comunero Revolution in Colombia, 1781 (1978)

events.certainty_note: Revolt dates and negotiation documents well-preserved in colonial archives.

events.title: Haidar Ali's Mysore forces ravage the Carnatic
events.description: Throughout 1781, Hyder Ali's forces, in alliance with the French under the Bailli de Suffren who arrived in Indian waters, continued to devastate the Carnatic region around Madras. The destruction of agricultural land and displacement of populations created a humanitarian catastrophe that contemporary British officials compared to the worst famines. The British managed to hold Madras and win tactical engagements, but Mysorean strategic pressure demonstrated the fragility of East India Company territorial power and contributed to Edmund Burke's growing critique of Company misrule.
key_figures: Hyder Ali (Sultan of Mysore)
Bailli de Suffren (French admiral in the Indian Ocean)

sources.name: East India Company correspondence, Madras Presidency records, 1781
Sanjay Subrahmanyam, Penumbral Visions (2001)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in Company records and French naval dispatches; Suffren arrived in early 1782, not 1781 — his influence is more properly a 1782 event, but Mysore-French coordination was already in negotiation.

events.title: Tenmei famine conditions begin in Japan
events.description: By 1781, the early signs of what would become the devastating Tenmei famine (1782–1788) were emerging in northern Honshū. Unusual cold weather, possibly linked to volcanic activity (Mount Asama would erupt catastrophically in 1783), produced poor harvests in the Tōhoku region. The famine, when it fully struck, killed hundreds of thousands and triggered rice riots and social unrest that challenged the stability of the Tokugawa shogunate. The crisis exposed the vulnerabilities of Japan's rice-based economy and led to reform initiatives under senior councillor Tanuma Okitsugu.
key_figures: Tanuma Okitsugu (senior councillor, rōjū, pursuing mercantilist reforms)
Tokugawa Ieharu (10th shōgun)

sources.name: Domain (han) records from Tōhoku provinces documenting crop yields
Conrad Totman, Early Modern Japan (1993)

events.certainty_note: The onset of poor harvests in 1781 is attested in regional records, but the famine is conventionally dated from 1782. Assigning the beginning to 1781 is based on agricultural data rather than a single event.

events.title: Completion of the Iron Bridge at Coalbrookdale
events.description: The Iron Bridge, the first major bridge in the world constructed entirely of cast iron, was opened to traffic on 1 January 1781 at Coalbrookdale, Shropshire. Designed by Thomas Farnolls Pritchard and cast at Abraham Darby III's foundry, the bridge spanned the River Severn with a single arch of 100 feet. It became an iconic symbol of the Industrial Revolution, demonstrating the structural possibilities of iron as a building material and attracting visitors from across Europe. The Coalbrookdale area, already a centre of iron production, is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
key_figures: Abraham Darby III (ironmaster who oversaw construction)
Thomas Farnolls Pritchard (architect, died 1777 before completion)

sources.name: Contemporary accounts in the Shrewsbury Chronicle and Gentleman's Magazine, 1781
Barrie Trinder, The Industrial Revolution in Shropshire (3rd ed., 2000)

events.certainty_note: Opening date of 1 January 1781 confirmed by contemporary press accounts, though the bridge had been substantially complete since late 1779.

disconfirming_evidence: Several points of dispute and complication: (1) The significance of Yorktown as 'the battle that ended the war' is somewhat oversimplified — significant fighting continued in the South, the Caribbean, India, and at sea through 1782–83, and the British still held New York, Charleston, and Savannah. The North ministry did not fall until March 1782, and peace negotiations were protracted. (2) Túpac Katari's famous last words ('I will return as millions') are widely quoted but their authenticity is contested by historians; they may be a later nationalist invention. (3) The founding date of Los Angeles (4 September 1781) is the traditional date used in civic celebrations, but some scholars argue the actual settlement process was more gradual and that the specific date reflects administrative formality rather than a single founding event. (4) The Iron Bridge opening is sometimes dated to 1779 (when the structure was completed) rather than 1781 (when it was opened to paying traffic); the distinction matters for claims about 'first' iron bridges. (5) Herschel's discovery of Uranus on 13 March 1781 was not immediately recognized as a planet — Herschel himself reported it as a comet, and its planetary nature was not confirmed until orbital calculations were completed months later, raising questions about what date properly counts as the 'discovery.'
historiographic_note: 1781 is exceptionally well-documented from European and Euro-American perspectives, with rich archives from British, French, Spanish, American, and Habsburg sources. The American Revolution dominates Anglophone historiography, potentially crowding out equally consequential events like the Andean uprisings and the Second Anglo-Mysore War. South American events are documented primarily through Spanish colonial archives and trial records, which carry inherent biases against indigenous perspectives. The Tây Sơn period in Vietnam is documented largely through later Nguyễn-dynasty histories, which were hostile to the Tây Sơn and must be read critically. Japanese domain records provide good agricultural data but limited political narrative. Sub-Saharan African and Pacific events remain poorly attested in the sources available.

graph_edges.note: Yorktown surrender made American independence politically inevitable; Treaty of Paris followed in 1783
French naval victory at the Chesapeake directly enabled the siege of Yorktown by blocking British relief
Cowpens forced Cornwallis into aggressive pursuit of Greene, leading to Guilford Court House
Pyrrhic victory at Guilford Court House prompted Cornwallis to march to Virginia, where he was trapped at Yorktown
Túpac Amaru II's execution and Túpac Katari's siege of La Paz were phases of the same Andean uprising
Andean and Comunero revolts were parallel anti-Bourbon uprisings across Spanish America in 1780–81
Toleranzpatent and abolition of serfdom were both components of Joseph II's Enlightenment reform programme
Kant's Critique and Joseph's reforms both expressed the Enlightenment's high tide in German-speaking Europe
The Articles of Confederation's structural weaknesses led directly to the 1787 Constitutional Convention
Early harvest failures in 1781 worsened catastrophically after the 1783 eruption of Mount Asama
Spanish capture of Pensacola was part of the broader Allied campaign that culminated at Yorktown
British engagements in the Carnatic and Mysore's devastation of the region were concurrent theaters of the same war
